corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: In this work, we have proposed SPLADE, a sparse model revisiting query/document expansion.

location.sentence: In this work, we present a new first-stage ranker based on explicit sparsity regularization and a log-saturation effect on term weights, leading to highly sparse representations and competitive results with respect to state-of- the-art dense and sparse methods.

context_window.previous: Meanwhile, there has been a growing interest in learning sparse representations for documents and queries, that could inherit from the desirable properties of bag-of-words models such as the exact matching of terms and the efficiency of inverted indexes.
context_window.current: In this work, we present a new first-stage ranker based on explicit sparsity regularization and a log-saturation effect on term weights, leading to highly sparse representations and competitive results with respect to state-of- the-art dense and sparse methods.
context_window.next: Our approach is simple, trained end-to-end in a single stage.

location.sentence: However, as the vocabulary associated with a document remains the same, this type of approach does not solve the vocabu- lary mismatch, as acknowledged by the use of query expansion for retrieval [ 4 ].

context_window.previous: Based on BERT, DeepCT [ 4 – 6 ] focused on learning contextualized term weights in the full vocabulary space – akin to BOW term weights.
context_window.current: However, as the vocabulary associated with a document remains the same, this type of approach does not solve the vocabu- lary mismatch, as acknowledged by the use of query expansion for retrieval [ 4 ].
context_window.next: A first solution to this problem consists in expanding documents using generative approaches such as doc2query [ 19 ] and docTTTTTquery [ 18 ] to predict expansion words for documents.

location.sentence: Given a query 𝑞 𝑖 in a batch, a positive docu- ment 𝑑 + 𝑖 , a (hard) negative document 𝑑 − 𝑖 (e.g. coming from BM25 sampling), and a set of negative documents in the batch (positive documents from other queries) { 𝑑 − 𝑖,𝑗 } 𝑗 , we consider the ranking loss from [ 8 ], which can be interpreted as the maximization of the prob- ability of the document 𝑑 + 𝑖 being relevant among the documents 𝑑 + 𝑖 , 𝑑 − 𝑖 and { 𝑑 − 𝑖,𝑗 } : L 𝑟𝑎𝑛𝑘 − 𝐼 𝐵𝑁 = − log 𝑒 𝑠 ( 𝑞 𝑖 ,𝑑 + 𝑖 ) 𝑒 𝑠 ( 𝑞 𝑖 ,𝑑 + 𝑖 ) + 𝑒 𝑠 ( 𝑞 𝑖 ,𝑑 − 𝑖 ) + Í 𝑗 𝑒 𝑠 ( 𝑞 𝑖 ,𝑑 − 𝑖,𝑗 ) (5) The in-batch negatives (IBN) sampling strategy is widely used for training image retrieval models, and has shown to be effective in learning first-stage rankers [8, 12, 15].

top_candidates.sentence: We compare to the following sparse approaches (1) BM25 (2) DeepCT (3) doc2query-T5 (4) and SparTerm , as well as state-of-the-art dense approaches ANCE and TCT-ColBERT .

Overall, we observe that: (1) our models outperform the other sparse retrieval methods by a large margin (except for recall@1000 on TREC DL); (2) the results are competitive with state-of-the-art dense retrieval methods.

Overall, we observe that: (1) our models outperform the other sparse retrieval methods by a large margin (except for recall@1000 on TREC DL); (2) the results are competitive with state-of-the-art dense retrieval methods.

More specifically, our training method for ST lexical-only already outperforms the results of DeepCT as well as the results reported in the original SparTerm paper -including the model using expansion.
Thanks to the additional sparse expansion mechanism, we are able to obtain results on par with state-of-the-art dense approaches on MS MARCO dev set (e.g.

It is also remarkably simple, compared to dense state-of-the-art baselines such as ANCE , and avoids resorting to approximate neighbors search, whose impact on IR metrics has not been fully evaluated yet.
In the following, we propose slight, but essential changes to the SparTerm model that dramatically improve its performance.
We trained and evaluated our models on the MS MARCO passage ranking dataset 1 in the full ranking setting.
The dataset contains approximately 8.8M passages, and hundreds of thousands training queries with shallow annotation (≈ 1.1 relevant passages per query in average).
We keep the best checkpoint using MRR@10 on a validation set of 500 queries, after training for 150k iterations (note that this is not optimal, as we validate on a re-ranking task).
We report Recall@1000 for both datasets, as well as the official metrics MRR@10 and NDCG@10 for MS MARCO dev set and TREC DL 2019 respectively.
We report Recall@1000 for both datasets, as well as the official metrics MRR@10 and NDCG@10 for MS MARCO dev set and TREC DL 2019 respectively.

Overall, we observe that: (1) our models outperform the other sparse retrieval methods by a large margin (except for recall@1000 on TREC DL); (2) the results are competitive with state-of-the-art dense retrieval methods.
Recall@1000 close to 0.96 for ST exp-ℓ 1 ), but with a much bigger average number of FLOPS.

Experiments show that the expansion brings improvements w.r.t. to the purely lexical approach by increasing recall.
We introduce a minor change in the importance estimation from Eq.

In this work, we present a new first-stage ranker based on explicit sparsity regularization and a log-saturation effect on term weights, leading to highly sparse representations and competitive results with respect to state-ofthe-art dense and sparse methods.
At the beginning of 2019, Nogueira and Cho achieved state-of-the-art results -by a large margin -on the MS MARCO passage re-ranking task, paving the way for LM-based neural ranking models.

The contributions of this paper are threefold: (1) we build upon SparTerm , and show that a mild tuning of hyperparameters brings improvements that largely outperform the results reported in the original paper; we propose the SParse Lexical AnD Expansion (SPLADE) model, based on a logarithmic activation and sparse regularization.

The contributions of this paper are threefold: (1) we build upon SparTerm , and show that a mild tuning of hyperparameters brings improvements that largely outperform the results reported in the original paper; we propose the SParse Lexical AnD Expansion (SPLADE) model, based on a logarithmic activation and sparse regularization.

While the backbone of these models remains the same, recent works highlight the critical aspects of the training strategy to obtain state-of-the-art results, ranging from improved negative sampling to distillation .

SPLADE is an appealing candidate for initial retrieval: it rivals the latest state-of-the-art dense retrieval models, its training procedure is straightforward, its sparsity/FLOPS can be controlled explicitly through the regularization, and it can operate on inverted indexes.
